brief_justification: Answered Yes but correct answer is No, so wrong answer with plausible but misapplied reasoning; Goes beyond surface with conjunctive causation analysis and counterfactual reasoning but misreads the folk psychology literature on symmetric joint causation; Applies inversion and bidirectionality checks showing some self-monitoring of reasoning process; Performs counterfactual and structural checks but they led to the wrong conclusion; Presents answer with confidence, only mild hedging about shared causation; Mentions symmetric overdetermination but doesn't seriously evaluate why people might say No (norm violations, abnormality focus); Shows derivation steps clearly but the folk psychology claim is asserted without evidence for the specific symmetric case.
